segments.s001-g1: به نام is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning “in the name of”; here به introduces the name of the one addressed. خداوند is the head noun and is followed by two nouns in apposition (جان و خرد) that specify attributes: “Lord of Soul and (of) Wisdom.” The sequence is nominal and no copula appears because this is an invocation formula.
segments.s001-g2: کز is the classical contracted relative (که + از) introducing a relative clause meaning “who/whose/than whom.” این is the demonstrative “this” (referring to the preceding epithet), برتر is a comparative/adjectival predicate “higher/superior,” and اندیشه is the noun “thought.” بر + نگذرد forms a verbal phrase: بر here means “over/above,” and نگذرد is the present (jussive/indicative negative) of گذشتن (‘to pass/exceed’) meaning “does not pass / is not surpassed.” The whole clause says that no thought surpasses (is superior to) this one.
segments.s001-g3: This is a string of epithets built by juxtaposing خداوند with nominal complements: خداوند نام = “Lord of name” (i.e. Lord of renown) and خداوند جای = “Lord of place/station.” Persian poetry commonly places the modifying noun after the head noun without an explicit genitive particle, so these are appositional compounds.
segments.s001-g4: Again خداوند begins a nominal series followed by two agent-nouns: روزی‌ده (“sustenance-giver,” literally ‘one who gives روزی’) and رهنمای (“guide”). There is no copula (است) explicitly because Persian poetic style lists attributes/appellations; the meaning is “God — provider of sustenance and guide.”
segments.s001-g5: خداوند again heads a list of cosmic titles. کیوان (Keyvān) is a planetary name (here the planet Saturn) and گردان‌سپهر is a compound noun ‘revolving heaven(s)’ or ‘the turning firmament.’ The conjunction و links the two complements to خداوند, forming cosmic epithets.
segments.s001-g6: فروزنده is an agent/adjectival noun meaning “illuminating/brightening” (from فروزانیدن), governing a series of objects: ماه (the Moon), ناهید (Venus or the goddess Anahita/Nahid), and مهر (here a proper-name deity, Mithra/Mehr). The list connected by و are the things he illumines; the verb/copula is again omitted because this is a series of epithets.
segments.s001-g7: ز is the poetic form of از (“from/of”) used to introduce the things compared: “from name and sign and conjecture.” The three nouns (نام، نشان، گمان) are coordinated by و. برتر است is a predicate: برتر (comparative/adjective “higher/superior”) plus the copula است, yielding “is superior to/above name, sign and conjecture.”
segments.s001-g8: This is a nominal sentence with نگارنده (“the writer/designer/inscriber”) as the subject and است as the copula. برشده modifies پیکر: برشده is a participial/adjectival form meaning “cut-out / fashioned / severed” and پیکر is “body/figure,” so the phrase states that the Shaper (نگارنده) is the one who fashioned the body. In context نگارنده functions as a title for the Creator/Artificer.
segments.s001-g25: پرستنده باشی و جوینده‌راه is a hortatory/subjunctive clause: باشی is a subjunctive/imperative-form copula meaning “be” (addressing the reader), and two nominal predicates are coordinated by و. Word order places the predicate nouns before the verb in poetic style: “be a worshipper and a seeker-of-the-way.”
segments.s001-g26: به ژرفی is an adverbial phrase (به + noun) meaning “deeply/in depth.” به فرمانش کردن نگاه is a verbal construction where فرمانش کردن is a gerund/nominalized verb phrase “doing/carrying out his command” (کردن as a light/auxiliary verb), and نگاه (gaze/look) combines with the preposition به to mean “look toward/regard.” The phrase literally means “look deeply toward doing his command.”
segments.s001-g27: توانا بود هر که دانا بود is a proverbial, inverted copular clause: بود is the past copula used generically (“was/ is”), and هر که introduces a relative clause “whoever/one who.” The structure reads literally “capable was whoever wise was,” i.e. “he who is wise is capable.”
segments.s001-g28: ز دانش is a causal/instrumental prepositional phrase “by/through knowledge.” دل پیر is the noun phrase “an old heart,” and برنا بود uses the past copula (بود) to state the result: the old heart “was/ became youthful.” The verb is omitted as an agentive causative; the sense is “through knowledge an old heart becomes young.”
segments.s001-g29: از این پرده برتر is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning “beyond this veil” (از ... برتر used poetically). سخن‌گاه is a compound noun “place/seat of speech”; نیست negates existence: “there is no place for speech beyond this veil.”
segments.s001-g30: ز هستی opens a frame meaning “with regard to/from existence.” مر is the dative/benefactive pronoun “me/for me,” اندیشه را is the definite object “thought,” and راه نیست is the predicate “there is no path/way.” Together: “as for existence, thought has no way/path to me.” Note را marks اندیشه as the definite object of the situation.
segments.s001-g9: به بینندگان آفریننده را is an object phrase placed before the verbs that follow. به marks the indirect object/recipient ('to/for the beholders'), بینندگان is the plural 'beholders/viewers', آفریننده را is the definite direct object ('the Creator' + راستۀِ object marker را). The verb that acts on these objects appears in the next segment.
segments.s001-g10: نبینی is a 2nd-person singular negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'you do not see' and here introduces a conditional sense ('if you do not see'). مرنجان is a negative imperative ('do not anger / do not grieve' — from رنجاندن, with the negative prefix). دو بیننده را is the definite object ('the two beholders' + را). Together the clause reads as a conditional + command: 'If you do not see ..., do not anger the two beholders.'
segments.s001-g11: نیابد is 3rd-person singular negative present ('does not find / cannot attain'). بدو is the dative 'to him' (be + o). نیز is an adverb 'also / likewise' modifying the clause. اندیشه راه is a noun–noun sequence where اندیشه ('thought') qualifies راه ('way'), i.e. 'the way of thought' or 'a way by which thought might reach'. The whole phrase means 'thought likewise finds no way to him.'
segments.s001-g12: که introduces an explanatory/reason clause here ('for / because'). او is the pronoun 'he'. برتر از is the comparative 'higher/superior to' (برتر + از). نام و از جایگاه lists the two terms being compared: نام ('name / reputation') and جایگاه ('place / status'). The second از repeats the comparative relation: 'higher than name and than station.'
segments.s001-g13: سخن is 'speech/words'. هر چه is the correlative 'whatever / whatever that', introducing a free relative clause. زین is an archaic/contractional form (here 'از این' = 'of/from these') modifying گوهران ('gems/essences'); together زین گوهران = 'of these gems'. بگذرد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive from گذرادن/گذریدن, used here in the sense 'pass by / surpass / go beyond.' The clause means 'whatever speech passes beyond these gems.'
segments.s001-g14: نیابد (3rd-person negative) 'does not find / cannot attain'. بدو 'to him'. راه 'the way / path'. جان و خرد 'soul and wisdom' (two terms joined): the phrase means 'the way of soul and wisdom finds no way to him' — i.e. 'soul and intellect cannot reach him.' The verb NIYĀBAD applies to the whole noun phrase following it.
segments.s001-g15: خرد‌گر ('the wise one' or 'one who has wisdom') is the subject. سخن is the object 'words / speech'. برگزیند is a present/habitual 3rd-person form meaning 'chooses / selects' (بر- prefix + گزیدن). همی is a classical adverb meaning 'always / habitually' and here marks the repeated/habitual action: 'the wise man chooses words (habitually)'. Word order is subject + object + verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s001-g16: همان را is the definite object 'that same (thing)'. گزیند is another present form of 'choose' ('he chooses / selects'). که is the relative pronoun 'that/which'. بیند is present 'he sees' (from دیدن) and همی again gives habitual/emphatic sense. Together: 'he chooses that very thing which he sees (i.e. perceives) always.'
segments.s001-g17: ستودن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to praise' placed at the start to mean 'how to praise' or 'praising'; نداند is the archaic present negative 'knows not' (subject follows); کس here functions as the negative indefinite 'no one'; او را is the definite object 'him' (را marks it); چو هست = 'as he is' (چو = 'as/like', هست = copula). Word order is poetic (Infinitive + verb + subject + object + subordinate simile).
segments.s001-g18: میان بندگی را is a noun phrase 'the midst/middle of servitude' with را marking it as definite/objective; ببایدت is the impersonal necessity construction with enclitic ت = 'for you' ('it is necessary for you'); بست is the verb 'to bind' (here the required action after the necessity). Together they mean 'you must bind [yourself] in the midst of servitude.'
segments.s001-g19: خرد را و جان را places two definite objects ('the mind' and 'the soul') with را after each; همی is the frequentative adverb 'continually/still' that precedes the verb; سنجد here is the verb from سنجیدن 'he weighs/tests/examines' (the provided sense list lacks this verbal sense); اوی is the emphatic subject pronoun 'he' placed after the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s001-g20: در اندیشه سخته is a prepositional phrase 'in/within hard/difficult thought' (سخته post-modifies اندیشه in poetic order); کی is the interrogative 'when' here; گنجد is the present of گنجیدن 'to fit / be contained' and اوی again is the pronoun 'he' (verb before pronoun in poetry), so 'in hard thought, when will he fit/be contained?'
segments.s001-g21: بدین (here split as ب + دین in the word list) functions as the prepositional 'by/with these'; آلت is 'instrument' and is followed by the list رأی و جان و زبان ('reason/opinion, life/soul, and tongue/speech') which together specify the instruments. Word order is tersely enumerative in poetic style.
segments.s001-g22: ستود is the verb 'to praise' placed before its object (poetic inversion); آفریننده را = 'the Creator' with را marking the definite object; کی is the interrogative 'who' here, and توان is the modal 'can/able'. The clause literally reads 'Praise the Creator — who can?' i.e. 'Who can praise the Creator?'
segments.s001-g23: به هستیش is a prepositional phrase 'with/regarding his being' (هستیش = هست + enclitic ـش 'his being/existence'); باید introduces necessity and is followed by که plus the subordinate clause; خستو شوی is subjunctive/optative 'you become weary/humbled' (شوی, present subjunctive of شدن with an implicit 'thou').
segments.s001-g24: ز گفتار بیکار is an ablative phrase 'from/away from idle/useless speech' (ز = 'from', گفتار = 'speech', بیکار = 'idle/useless'); یک‌سو شوی is the verb phrase 'become one-sided / turn aside' where شوی is again subjunctive/optative governed by the earlier باید — 'you must withdraw to one side.'

sentences.s001-s1: In the name of God, Lord of Soul and Wisdom — whose thought nothing higher than this can surpass.
sentences.s001-s2: Lord of name and Lord of station; Lord, bestower of sustenance and guide.
sentences.s001-s3: Lord of Keyvān and of the revolving heavens, illuminator of the Moon, of Nahid and of Mehr.
sentences.s001-s4: He is above name and token and conjecture; the Shaper is the one who fashioned the body.
sentences.s001-s13: Be a worshipper and a seeker of the Way; look deeply toward doing his command.
sentences.s001-s14: Able is one who is wise; through knowledge an old heart becomes young.
sentences.s001-s15: Beyond this veil there is no place for speech; with respect to being, thought has no path to me.
sentences.s001-s5: If the beholders do not see the Creator, do not anger the two beholders.
sentences.s001-s6: Thought likewise finds no way to him, for he is above name and station.
sentences.s001-s7: Whatever speech surpasses these gems, the way of soul and wisdom cannot find its way to him.
sentences.s001-s8: A wise man, when choosing words, always selects the very thing he perceives.
sentences.s001-s9: No one knows how to praise Him as He is; in the midst of servitude you must bind yourself.
sentences.s001-s10: He continually tests the mind and the soul; in severe thought, when will he be contained?
sentences.s001-s11: By these instruments — reason, life, and tongue — who can praise the Creator?
sentences.s001-s12: Because of His being you must become humbled; from idle speech you must withdraw to one side.
